coredata.dc:title: Highly selective antenna effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs): A new fluorescent sensitizer for rare earth element terbium in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shengnan
Chu, Xu
Xiang, Xiaoyan
Cao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: This study focused on the fluorescence antenna-sensitizing effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in a case on the detection of terbium ions. A simple one-step chemical oxidation method was applied for the preparation of GQDs starting from the regular multilayer graphene oxide (GO) via the refluxing in a concentrated mixture of strong acids. The as-prepared GQDs were further evaluated as a fluorescent sensitizer to the terbium ion. An expanded in-deep mechanism study on the fluorescence phenomena during the interaction of the as-prepared GQDs and REEs was made. The highly selective antenna effect of GQDs on one of REEs’ aqueous media, which was terbium (III), was identified. The excited terbium ion emitted its long-living fluorescence based on its own characteristic line-typed f-f transition, contrasting to a undetectable fluorescence in a very poor quantum yield in its aqueous solution induced by water collisions. This study, in the first place, identified the significant sensitization effect of the as-prepared GQDs on the terbium ion in a high selectivity in aqueous media. The detectable linear range and the detection limit of the terbium ion was 0–30 × 10−6 mol L−1 (R2 = 0.9960) of 0.3 × 10−6 mol L−1, respectively. The excitation wavelength and the optimal fluorescence wavelength were 230 nm 546 nm, respectively. Further material characterizations, involving XPS, FTIR, Raman and the Zeta potential, verified the important participation of carboxyl function groups on the as-prepared GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Antenna-sensitizing effect
Graphene quantum dots
Terbium
Rare earth elements

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Fluorescent determination of rare earth elements
Analysis of Tb in terms of the linear range and the detection limit

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The low-magnification TEM of the as-prepared GQDs (inset the HRTEM images) (b) Size distribution of the as-prepared GQDs.
XRD patter of the as-prepared GQDs.
(a) FTIR spectra of the as-prepared GQDs and GO, (b) the Raman spectra of as-prepared GQDs.
(a) Full survey spectrum of as-prepared GQDs. (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of O 1s.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs (red line) 、GQDs-Tb solution (blue line), and GQDs-Tb solution (cyan line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(a) Fluorescence spectra change of the as-prepared GQDs in the presence of the REE ions at 230 nm excitation. (b) The fluorescence intensity of the fluorescence emission spectra in 546 nm.

(a) The change of the fluorescence emission spectra of the as-prepared GQDs with different concentration of Tb3+ (0–250 × 10−6 mol L−1). (b) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs at 546 nm and the concentration of Tb3+.

Fluorescence response of the GQDs 0.025 mg/mL) aqueous solutions at 546 nm in the presence of different rare earth ions. (Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+,Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, about 5 × 10−6 mol L−1).

(a) High resolution C 1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared GQDs and GQDs-Tb. (b) High resolution O 1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared GQDs and GQDs-Tb. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of Tb 3d of GQDs-Tb solution.
Schematic diagram illustrating the conceptive energy level and the energy transfer processes in Tb-GQDs and the fluorescence spectra (a).
The process of the as-prepared GQDs.

head.ce:title: Highly selective antenna effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs): A new fluorescent sensitizer for rare earth element terbium in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY, 42101, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Combustion Science and Environmental Technology, Department of Chemistry, Western Kentucky University, Bowling Green, KY 42101, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei, 230601, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study focused on the fluorescence antenna-sensitizing effect of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in a case on the detection of terbium ions. A simple one-step chemical oxidation method was applied for the preparation of GQDs starting from the regular multilayer graphene oxide (GO) via the refluxing in a concentrated mixture of strong acids. The as-prepared GQDs were further evaluated as a fluorescent sensitizer to the terbium ion. An expanded in-deep mechanism study on the fluorescence phenomena during the interaction of the as-prepared GQDs and REEs was made. The highly selective antenna effect of GQDs on one of REEs’ aqueous media, which was terbium (III), was identified. The excited terbium ion emitted its long-living fluorescence based on its own characteristic line-typed f-f transition, contrasting to a undetectable fluorescence in a very poor quantum yield in its aqueous solution induced by water collisions. This study, in the first place, identified the significant sensitization effect of the as-prepared GQDs on the terbium ion in a high selectivity in aqueous media. The detectable linear range and the detection limit of the terbium ion was 0–30 × 10−6 mol L−1 (R2 = 0.9960) of 0.3 × 10−6 mol L−1, respectively. The excitation wavelength and the optimal fluorescence wavelength were 230 nm 546 nm, respectively. Further material characterizations, involving XPS, FTIR, Raman and the Zeta potential, verified the important participation of carboxyl function groups on the as-prepared GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Rare earth elements (REEs) refer to lanthanides of 14 elements, and also scandium and yttrium. REEs have wide and strategic applications [1–3]. Since their low concentrations distributing in the earth's crust, their mining, extraction and purification address on the intensive energy and the significant environmental impacts. This makes their analytical determination especially critical. In contrast, the close similarity of REEs in their chemical properties make their distinguish and further quantitative determination a great challenge in analytical chemistry [4].Terbium, an important REE, has very low concentration in a level of only 1 ppm in the earth's crust. Its unique electronic layout state determines its critical applications in the fluorescence lamp, color television, catalysis and optical actuators. For example, its fluorescence emissions have high sensitivity and quick response time. Many methods have been developed to analytically determine Terbium, but incurring issues in their bulky, higher sample consumption, harmful chemical disposal, high cost, and lack of repeatability [5]. The fluorescence method on the analytical determination of Terbium shows advantages in portability, convenience, rapidness, safety, economic and also likelihood of the in-situ setup.

The fluorescence method generally requires fluorescent agents, which enhances fluorescence effect of target materials, especially those fluorescent agents (sensors) for metal ions. Thus, there is an increasing interest in the development of the convenient and inexpensive, most importantly highly quantum-efficient, fluorescent sensors and sensitizers [6]. Fluorescent sensors and sensitizers not only greatly contribute to the quick development of fluorescent spectroscopic techniques, also to other areas such as renewable solar energy harvesting. Among the various detection techniques, carbon nanomaterial-based fluorescent sensors or sensitizers have been quickly developed for their abundance, accessibility, cheapness, safety, and high sensitivity. The carbon nanomaterial family includes famous members such as fullerenes, graphene, carbon dots, nanotube and nanodiamonds [7]. Among of them, carbon dots refer to the carbon nanoparticles sizing in less than 10 nm with unique quantum effects, which always include carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the polymer dots. GQDs, and further regular CQDs, have attracted much attention in various field due to their unique characteristics, such as the excellent solubility, high sensitivity and low toxicity. On the other hand, their quantum size effects and excellent electron donor and acceptors arise excellent optical properties, especially in areas of the fluorescent imaging and sensing [8–11]. The application of GQDs and CQDs firstly addressed on their preparation, which generally include two basic methods of the top-down and the bottom-up [12]. The former involved the cutting of the graphene sheets, such as the concentrated acid oxidation [13,14], the electrochemical oxidation [15], the microwave auxiliary oxidation [16], and etc. The latter involved the direct synthesis of GQDs containing a certain mummer of conjugated carbon atoms starting from the small molecules [17–19]. As expected, the as-prepared GQDs or CQDs have the large surface, and also usually contain rich surface carbon functional groups. The richest ones are carboxylic groups which can be electron acceptors and thus can be instantly conjugated to metal ions [20]. Noteworthy, reports on the distinguishing and detection of REE ions using GQDs and CQDs were found especially rare, only involving a few such as one of our published work [21]. The GQDs or CQDs, as ligands, can absorb the light energy, and transfer energy to REE ions, and REE ion receive energy to emit characteristic fluorescence. This process is so-called the “antenna effect”. Therefore, the drawback of REEs ions in their weak absorptive and poor quantum yield can be avoided by the GQDs or CQDs. To our best knowledge, there are no further studies on the sensitizing effect or the antenna effect of GQDs or CQDs on the detection of REEs, especially Terbium.

This study further explored potentials of the sensitizing effect or the antenna effect in fluorescence regarding the use of the as-prepared GQDs on the detection of terbium ions. For the simplicity, a simple one-step chemical oxidation method was applied for the preparation of GQDs starting from the regular commercial multilayer graphene oxide (GO) via the refluxing in the concentrated mixture of strong acids. The resulting GQDs were further evaluated as a fluorescent sensitizer to all REEs including the terbium ion. An expanded in-deep mechanism studies addressed on material characterizations of the as-prepared GQDs and fluorescence phenomena during the interaction of the as-prepared GQDs and terbium ions.

Multilayer graphene oxide (GO) was purchased from Tan Feng Tech.inc. (China). The nitrate salts of 16 rare earth metal ions (Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+) used in this study were purchased from the Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Standard stock solutions of REE ions were prepared using the ultrapure water from the respective REE nitrate salts. Concentrated sulfuric acid (98%), concentrated nitric acid (68%) and sodium hydroxide were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Limited Corporation (China). All chemical reagents were commercially available, and used without further purification. The dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) was by from Shanghai (china).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared learning to our previous publication [22]. In brief, GQDs were synthesized by refluxing the commercial GO suspension in mixture of concentrated acids at the ambient condition. In a typical synthesis process, about 0.5 g of the commercial GO was added into the mixture of acids (the sulfuric acid and nitric acid). The solution was stirred at a temperature of 110 °C for 24 h in an oil bath. The solution was cooled and poured into a beaker with ultrapure ice water (800 mL). The pH of solution was adjusted to neutral (pH = 7–8) with sodium hydroxide salts to neutralize the unreacted acids. The final product solution was filtered was filtered off using a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove large or unreacted multilayer graphene oxide. The final product solution was further dialyzed against purification water in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) for 7 days. Finally, the clear, light, brown GQDs aqueous was concentrated using a rotary evaporation (RE-52AA) and dried in a Freeze Drying Machine (Lab-1-80) to obtain solid GQDs.

The as-prepared GQDs and their derived GQDs-REEs were characterized using several advanced instruments. The microstructures and the size distribution of GQDs were obtained using the High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM, JEM-2100 microscope), with an accelerating voltage of 150 kV. The chemical bonds of GQDs and GO were recorded using an FTIR Prestige-21 in the frequency range of 4000 to 500 cm−1. The chemical bonds and components of GQDs and GQDs-Tb were tested using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was tested using the Smart Lab 9 KW diffract meter, with Cu Kα radiation and a scanning rate of 10°/min (40 kV, 40 mA). The Zeta potential of GQDs was measured using a ZETASZER ZS90. The Ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometry (UV–Vis) and the fluorescent spectra (FL) were performed using a Shimadzu UV 3600 spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-2500 (Japan), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of rare earth elements
ce:section.ce:para: The affinity and selectivity of all 16 REEs on GQDs were studied by means of the fluorescence technique. It was initialized by the solution preparation, the addition of the rare earth element ions in to as-prepared GQDs solution (0.025 mg/mL) at desired concentrations, which was maintained to be fully reacted for 30 min at the ambient condition. This was followed by the measurement of fluorescence spectra of the mixtures. The appropriate excitation wavelength used in fluorescence spectra of GQD-REE solutions was 230 nm, and their unusual emission wavelengths were recorded at 490 nm, 546 nm, 585 nm and 620 nm. All sample measurements were performed in the aqueous media.

The as-prepared GQDs were simply synthesized by refluxing in the mixtures of concentrated sulfuric acid and the nitric acid for 24 h at 110 OC in an oil bath. The synthetic procedure of the as-prepared GQDs has been illustrated in Scheme 1 . The TEM images of the as-prepared GQDs were show in Fig. 1 (a). It revealed the nanosheet morphology of the as-prepared GQDs with an average size at 2.5 nm and a narrow size range at 1.5–3.8 nm, which was further accounted and exhibited in Fig. 1(b). Fig. 1(a) inset exhibits lattice spacing in its HRTEM image. There were two visible lattice spacing, the one was measured to be about 0.226 nm likely attributing to the (100) facets of graphite [23] and the other was about 0.320 nm likely attributing to the (002) lattice spacing of graphene [24].

The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of the as-prepared GQDs was shown in Fig. 2 . It displayed an apparent broad peak at 21.3°, which can be ascribed to the (002) lattice spacing of graphene [25]. This was consistent to its HRTEM image and further confirmed the existence of the graphite structure of as-prepared GQDs. The surface electron density of the as-prepared GQDs was measured by the Zeta potential analyzer, and the result was shown in Fig. S1.

In order to determining surface state, functional groups and composition of the as-prepared GQDs, the characterization were measured by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy (Raman), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 3 (a) shows the FTIR spectra of the as-prepared GQDs (red line) and the raw material (blue line). The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared GQDs exhibited much different from that of its raw material, in its obvious broaden absorption band at around 3400 cm−1, attributing to the hydroxyl (-OH) functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared GQDs. There many other changes in intensities at 1610 cm−1, 1420 cm−1, 1250 cm−1 and 1080 cm−1, corresponding to the CC special stretching vibration of aromatic hydrocarbon [26], C–H bending vibration [27], the C–O–C asymmetric and stretching vibration, respectively [28]. FTIR evidenced the significant appearance of carbon and oxygen relative functional groups, such as CC, C–O–C and –CH2 on the as-received GQDs. Fig. 3(b) shows the Raman spectra of the as-prepared GQDs. It exhibited a clear peak evolved at 1367 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of the sp2 carbon networks (D band) and the graphite structure (G band) regarding its defects and discord [29], respectively. The Raman measurement also can further confirmed the existence of graphite structure of the as-prepared GQDs, Moreover, the intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) of the as-prepared GQDs was found to be 0.93, indicating a partial disordered graphite-structure of the as-prepared GQDs [30].

X-ray spectroscopy (XPS) can further defined the surface groups in their valence states and the element compositions in the as-prepared GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), two main peaks at 284.5 eV and 532.0 eV represented characteristic peaks of C1s and O1s, respectively, confirming the likelihood of presence of carbon-oxygen functional groups on surfaces of the carbon frame in the as-prepared GQDs. The relative atomic percentage of C and O in the as-prepared GQD was found to be 65.36% and 34.63%, respectively. Fig. 4(b) presents the high resolution spectrum of carbon in XPS spectra. Among of them, three distinct peaks at 284.79 eV, 286.20eV and 288.67 eV were assigned to the graphitic or aliphatic C–C/CC, C–O, CO (-COOH) [31], respectively. As shown in Fig. 4(c), two peaks at 532.30 eV and 531.60 eV were ascribed to C–O (C–O–C/C–OH) and CO (-COOH), respectively [32]. Combining FTIR analysis, XPS results further confirmed the abundance of the carbon-oxygen functional groups, implying its favorable for its combination with the target REE ions [21].

The optical properties of the as-prepared GQDs and the GQD-Tb solutions using the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and the fluorescence emissions of the GQD-Tb solutions. In Fig. 5 , the red line presented the UV–Vis of the as-prepared GQDs, the blue line and the cyan line presented UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the GQD-Tb. Fig. 5 implies that the addition of terbium ions in GQDs changed optical properties of the as-prepared GQD. The UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared GQDs presented three characteristic absorption peak at 360 nm, 280 nm and 230 nm, the former two peaks likely attributing to the typical absorption of a n-π* transition for the CO bond [33] and the latter one to a π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 (CC) [34]. As for the GQD-Tb solution, its absorption peaks at 360 nm and 280 were found to decrease, implying a interaction between Tb ions and GQDs in the water solution environment, likely the clear affinity of terbium ions to the oxygen donor atoms originated of the carboxylate groups [35]. Moreover, the absorption peak of GQD-Tb excited at 230 nm was found a clear blue shift by 20 nm, coupling an enhanced absorption at 210 nm. The new absorption peak can be surely attributed to the carbonyl bonded terbium complex [36]. As the addition of Tb ions into GQD and likely the formation of binding between them, the emission spectra of GQD-Tb solution showed four strong emission peaks at 490 nm, 546 nm, 585 nm and 620 nm. On the one hand, comparing with the fluorescence excitation wavelength, it can further certificated that the fluorescence of the four peak is the typical optical properties of GQD-Tb solution.

There were a few examples on uses of carbon or graphene quantum dots (CQDs or GQDs) as fluorescence sensor to detect metal ions or other molecules base on the fluorescence quenching effect [37–39]. As for REE metal ions, there was a more complex situation in which both REE ions and used GQDs involved fluorescence, but likely at different wavelengths and varied quantum conversion efficiencies. Thus, their characteristic fluorescence and likely interaction were investigated, based on the as-prepared GQDs in this study. The preliminary investigation on fluorescence of mixtures of the as-prepared GQDs, with individually involved all REE ions (Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+), was conducted. All fluorescence measurements were performed in aqueous media, ambient condition and the optimal excitation wavelength was found at 230 nm. Fig. 6 (a) shows all fluorescence spectra of those solutions of GQD-REE mixtures. Results clearly illustrated that the addition of only terbium ions into the as-prepared GQDs solution can remarkably increase fluorescence intensity in a wide wavelength range. Specially, the emitted fluorescence of GQD-Tb was found at four different wavelengths, which were 490 nm, 546 nm, 585 nm and 620 nm. Surprisingly, these four strong peaks can be attributed to the characteristic electronic structures of terbium ions [40], corresponding to transitions of 5D4-7F6, 5D4-7F5, 5D4-7F4, 5D4-7F3 of the terbium ion, respectively [41]. And the fluorescence intensity at 546 nm of the GQDs-Tb was found to be greatest. Fig. 6(b) summarized fluorescence intensities of all GQDs-REEs at 546 nm, clearly, the fluorescence intensity attributing to terbium ions was much greater than those of the other involved REE ions. This preliminary test implied two facts, the significant fluorescence of Tb itself in an aqueous solution, and a high selectivity of Tb using the as-prepared GQDs in terms of fluorescence of REE. The evolution of the fluorescence of the as-prepared GQD-Tb solution was further investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. S2. The pH dependence of GQD-Tb solution was given in Fig. S3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Tb in terms of the linear range and the detection limit
ce:section.ce:para: For further investigation on the fluorescence mechanism, the absorption spectra of terbium ions at different concentrations adding in the as-prepared GQDs solution (0.025 mg/mL−1) were shown in Fig. S4. The fluorescence titration test was conducted tested in the optimal condition to search its detection limit of the terbium ions. Result was shown in Fig. 7 (a) , revealing that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-Tb solution at all four characteristic peaks increased upon the increase of the concentration of terbium ion. It's clearly identified the linear range of the terbium ion concentration was within 0–30 × 10−6 mol L−1, as shown in Fig. 7(b). The linear curve can be obtained as Y = 582.27X+229.75 (the linear correlation R2 = 0.9960). Y is the fluorescence value at 546 nm, and X is the concentration of the terbium ion. Therefore, under the opposite condition, the calculated detection limit of the terbium ion was found as low as 0.3 × 10−6 mol L−1.

As an ion-selective optical sensor, studies on the exploration of its interference and matrix effects are critical. Since REEs naturally occurred simultaneously, the possible interfering REEs ions, other than Tb, were added into the as-prepared GQDs aqueous solution. These potential interfering REEs ions involved Sc3+, Y3+, La3+, Ce3+, Pr3+, Nd3+, Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Dy3+, Ho3+, Er3+, Tm3+, Yb3+, Lu3+. Results were shown in Fig. 8 , F is the fluorescence intensity of REE ions other than Tb which were added into the GQD-Tb solution, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of the GQD-Tb solution. It revealed that the as-prepared GQDs had a significant sensitizing effect only on the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+, and there was no observation of fluorescence changes of GQDs-Tb solution upon the addition of REE ions other than Tb (low about 5 × 10−6 mol L−1), comparing to that of GQDs-Tb solution.

Fig. 9 (a) and (b) display the changes in the high-resolution C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of the as-prepared GQD and the GQD-Tb solution. As shown in Fig. 9(a), the comparison to the C 1s spectra of the as-prepared GQDs revealed that, a new spectrum at 289.00 eV of the GQDs-Tb appeared, while the peak at 288.60 eV obviously deceased. The analysis illustrated that terbium ions had a stronger interaction with the oxygen atom in carboxyl on surfaces of the as-prepared GQDs. As shown in Fig. 9(b), the peak of O1s spectra corresponding to the band CO (531.6 eV) of the GQD-Tb decreased comparing to that of the as-prepared GQDs high-resolution. Therefore, changes of both spectra of C 1s and O 1s of the GQDs-Tb confirmed the strong interaction between oxygen atom of carboxyl in GQDs surfaces and the terbium ion. Fig. 9(c) shows the high-resolution Tb 3d spectra of the GQD-Tb solution. This high-resolution XPS results displayed that two main peaks at the binding energy of 1277.0 eV and 1242.5 eV can be ascribed to the characteristic Tb 3d3/2 and Tb 3d5/2 peaks of the terbium ions of the terbium oxides in the GQD-Tb solution. Fig. S5 shows that the TEM image of the as-prepared GQD–Tb mixed solution, further indicating the Tb binding to the as-prepared GQDs further made the agglomeration of the as-prepared GQD, that was much different from that the GQDs solution only.

Terbium ions in water are regularly expected fluorescence in a very weak intensity owing to its low molar absorption ability to receive the ultraviolet–visible radiation. This revealed its poor quantum yield ascribing to the intensive collision occurring between terbium ions and the water molecules in aqueous media. It was also known that terbium ions were likely combined with some proper ligands to enhance their fluorescence intensity [42]. In this study, the addition of the terbium into the as-prepared GQDs solution achieved significant luminescence of even trivalent terbium ions in aqueous media. The measured fluorescence of the terbium ion was found in a higher selectivity and the significant sensitization. It's clear that the as-prepared GQDs helped in overcoming drawbacks of the terbium in its poor absorptivity, likely through the formation of the terbium and the as-prepared GQDs and preventing from the effect from water molecules. Moreover, the addition of Tb-GQD combination enhanced the fluorescence intensity of terbium because of the retained efficient energy transfer from the as-prepared GQDs to terbium ions. Furthermore, the emission fluorescence was identified to be the characteristic line-typed f-f transition of the terbium ions themselves. Therefore, the as-prepared GQDs, in whatever functionalities and mechanisms, was like an aforementioned proper ligand.

Fig. 10 illustrates the occurrence of the energy levels and typical energy transfer process involving Tb-GQDs. There involved three basic steps to interpret so-called “antenna effect” in the energy transition. Firstly, GQDs electron absorbs the incoming light energy to be excited and then is transited to the excited state (S1). Secondly, the excited electron of GQDs in singlet state (S1) transfers their energy to the terbium ions which are consequently excited. Finally, the excited terbium ions emit their long-living fluorescence via the characteristic line-typed f-f transition. The spectra as show in Fig. 10(a).

In summary, the following conclusions can be made.
(1)
The as-prepared GQDs was successfully synthesized by a one-step chemical oxidation method starting from the regular commercial multilayer graphene oxide (GO) via the refluxing in a concentrated mixture of strong acids, involving the sulfuric acid and the nitric acid.
(2)
The fluorescence and the antenna-sensitizing effect of the resulting GQDs in the case on the detection of terbium ions were studied.
(3)
This study identified the significant antenna sensitization effect of the as-prepared GQDs on the detection of the terbium ion solution in a high selectivity.
(4)
The detectable linear range of the terbium ion concentration was found to be0-30 × 10−6 mol L−1 (R2 = 0.9960) in a detection limit of 0.3 × 10−6 mol L−1, based on the optimal fluorescence wavelength at 546 nm at an excitation at 230 nm.
(5)
Further mechanism studies verified the important participation of carboxyl function groups of the as-prepared GQDs to the characteristic line-typed f-f transition derived fluorescence emission of the terbium ion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: All authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21676001), and the National Key R&D Program of China (Grant No. 2017YFE0105500).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2019.120504.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Satellite-to-ground quantum key distribution
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.K. Liao, W.Q. Cai, W.Y. Liu, L. Zhang, Y. Li, J.G. Ren, J. Yin, Q. Shen, Y. Cao, Z.P. Li, F.Z. Li, X.W. Chen, L.H. Sun, J.J. Jia, J.C. Wu, X.J. Jiang, J.F. Wang, Y.M. Huang, Q. Wang, Y.L. Zhou, L. Deng, T. Xi, L. Ma, T. Hu, Q. Zhang, Y.A. Chen, N.L. Liu, X.B. Wang, Z.C. Zhu, C.Y. Lu, R. Shu, C.Z. Peng, J.Y. Wang, J.W. Pan, Satellite-to-ground quantum key distribution, Nature 549 (2017) 43-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyol-mediated C-dot formation showing efficient Tb3+/Eu3+ emission
Chem. Commun.
